conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're inquiring about.

Let me start by asking: Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me here in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me ask you: Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not. He finished school and isn't doing any courses or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility.

Are you responsible for Alex - meaning do you live with him and provide for his care and upbringing?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives with me and I take care of him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And just to confirm: Do you live in the UK (England, Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Let me read the official guidance to confirm the eligibility rules before giving you my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the information you've provided and the official guidance I've reviewed, I can now provide you with an assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Unfortunately, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

The reason is that Alex is 17 years old, which means he's over the standard age limit of 16. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19, this only applies if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished school and is not currently doing any courses or training, he doesn't meet this requirement.

If Alex decides to start approved education or training in the future (such as A-levels, college courses, or certain training programs), you would be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point, provided he's still under 20 years old.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. For children aged 16 or over, you'll normally still qualify if your child is under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. Alex is 17 years old and is not currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (England) and is responsible for their 17-year-old son Alex who lives with them. However, since Alex is over 16 years old and is not currently in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for him. Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 requires that they remain in approved education or training.
